definition: The highest-ranking head of a country during the feudal period.
A person with the power to rule over a region, usually below the king but sometimes holding actual power.
Another name for a king, often used to assert independence and immense power.
The mother of the king, usually highly respected and holding an important voice in the court.
The highest scorer in the ancient imperial court examination, considered the smartest person in the country.
The person who ranked second in the court examination, right after the First-rank Scholar.
The person who ranked third in the court examination during the feudal period.
Civilian officials who assisted the Hung Kings in managing important national affairs.
Locals leaders of tribes in ancient times who assisted the Hung Kings in overseeing lands.
The head of a large administrative region in ancient times, appointed by Northern dynasties to rule.
The supreme ruler of all Indochina during the French colonial period.
A trusted person appointed by the king to carry out a special mission.
A military officer commanding the army in a province during the Nguyen Dynasty.
The highest military rank, commanding the entire national army in ancient times.
A person who commands the army in battle.
A servant in the royal palace who looked after the daily life and needs of the royal family.
Senior officials who held very high positions and frequently discussed national affairs with the king.
A person who represented the king to communicate and negotiate with other countries.
A woman who becomes the king and head of a country.
The head of a tribe during the Van Lang - Au Lac period.
Wealthy and prestigious people with local authority in ancient times.
An official title for rulers of a large region during the Northern domination period.
An official tasked with supervising other officials and advising the king against wrongdoings.
A daughter of the king.
A son of the king.
The head of the state in modern Vietnam.
The leader of the Communist Party of Vietnam.
The highest military rank in the Vietnam People's Army (e.g., General Vo Nguyen Giap).
The supreme commander of all armed forces.
People who directly fight with weapons to protect the fatherland.
Mass armed forces who both work and protect their homeland.
A secret combat force that carries out surprise attacks within cities (e.g., Saigon Commandos).
People who deliver messages and guide soldiers secretly.
A force of young people who took the lead in difficult tasks like road opening and ammunition transport.
A noble title for mothers who made great sacrifices for the country.
A person in charge of political and ideological work within military units.

term: Hero of the People's Armed Forces
definition: A title for those who performed exceptionally outstanding feats of war.
Local armed forces using surprise and flexible combat tactics.
Members of youth organizations participating in small-scale revolutionary activities.
A precious bronze drum, symbol of the long-standing civilization of the ancient Vietnamese.
The legendary weapon of King An Duong Vuong, capable of shooting thousands of arrows at once.
A precious sword that Le Loi borrowed from Long Quan (Dragon Lord) to drive out the Ming army.
The robe worn by the king, usually embroidered with dragons using gold thread.
The most solemn seat reserved for the king during ceremonies or court meetings.
A seal made of gold belonging to the king, used to stamp important documents.
Wooden plates carved with reverse characters to print books in ancient times.
Administrative documents with comments written in red ink by the king.
A traditional flag with five colors, often seen at festivals and temples.
Systems of high walls and deep moats built to protect capitals or important locations.
A place established by the people to remember and worship heroes who contributed to the country.
Stone slabs carved with the names of successful candidates or used to record major events.
A small round boat woven from bamboo, used for traveling on water.
A simple transport vehicle pulled by cattle in ancient times.
Dense bamboo rows surrounding villages to block wind and protect against enemies.
Unique weapons planted by Ngo Quyen in the Bach Dang River to defeat the enemy.
Books with pages made of bronze, often used to record royal genealogy or king's orders.
Traditional Vietnamese attire, embodying grace and elegance.
A characteristic hat for sun and rain protection, closely associated with the image of Vietnamese people.
A poetic term referring to the noble origins of the Vietnamese people.
A legendary bird species often found on the surface of Dong Son bronze drums.
A familiar folk game for children in rural Vietnam.
An object for decoration and lighting during traditional festivals.
A document used by the king to issue important orders to the entire population.
A document used by the king or generals to call upon and encourage the people to fight enemies.
The status of a country being self-governing and not ruled by another country.
The ability to decide and manage one's own affairs.
The period when our country was occupied and ruled by Northern dynasties for nearly 1,000 years.
A collective action to rise up and fight against invaders to regain freedom for the country.
A great change, serving as a turning point in society.
A process of change to make things better and more efficient.
The act of a strong country occupying and ruling over another country.
The act of a weak country presenting precious gifts to a strong country to maintain peace.

term: Edict on the Transfer of the Capital
definition: A famous document by King Ly Thai To regarding the move of the capital to Thang Long.

term: Proclamation to the Officers (Hich tuong si)
definition: A heroic essay by Tran Hung Dao to inspire soldiers to fight the Mongol army.

term: Great Proclamation upon the Pacification of the Wu
definition: A declaration of independence written by Nguyen Trai after defeating the Ming army.

term: Mountains and Rivers of the Southern Empire
definition: A divine poem affirming the sovereignty of the Southern land, attributed to Ly Thuong Kiet.
A solemn document declaring that a nation is fully independent.
Fine human values created and preserved through many generations.
A high degree of material and spiritual development of human beings.
Fine habits and customs passed down from generation to generation.
Deep affection and actions taken to protect and build one's homeland.
Binding and helping each other to achieve common goals.
A strong spirit of not bowing down to difficulties and enemies.
Courage and willingness to sacrifice for a great cause.
The supreme and complete right of a nation over its territory.
Land, sea, and air areas under the jurisdiction of a nation.
The line dividing territory between nations.
Sea areas and islands belonging to the nation's sovereignty.
The act of negotiation and communication between countries.
The capital of the Van Lang state during the Hung Kings' era (now in Phu Tho).
The capital of the Au Lac state with famous ancient spiral-shaped citadels.
The first capital of the Dai Co Viet state after Dinh Bo Linh unified the country.
The old name of the capital Hanoi, named by King Ly Thai To.
The stone capital built by the Ho Dynasty (now in Thanh Hoa).
The old name of the ancient capital Hue, where the Nguyen royal court was located.
The old name of present-day Ho Chi Minh City.
The land where Le Loi's heroic uprising against the Ming army began.
The site of the famous battle that shook the world in 1954.
A majestic mountain range, home to the legendary trail that supported the South during the war.
A historical river where three resounding victories against Northern invaders took place.
The river where the decisive battle against the Song army took place during the Ly Dynasty.
A strategic narrow pass where our troops ambushed and killed the enemy general Lieu Thang.
The place where beloved Uncle Ho left to search for national salvation.
A cave where Uncle Ho lived and worked after returning to the country.
The name of the very first state of our Vietnamese ancestors.
The state following Van Lang, led by Thuc Phan An Duong Vuong.
The name of the country given by Ly Bi, wishing for eternal peace like ten thousand springs.
The name of our country during the Dinh and Early Le dynasties.
The name our country held for the longest time in history, starting from the Ly Dynasty.
The official name of Vietnam during the reign of King Minh Mang.
The country's name after the August Revolution in 1945.
The official name of our country from 1976 to the present.
A stage in human history when people began to cast items out of bronze.
The earliest stage when humans mainly used stone to make labor tools.
A powerful armored vehicle, most famous being Tank 390 which crashed through the gates of Independence Palace.
Systems of secret tunnels underground used for living and fighting (e.g., Cu Chi Tunnels).
A characteristic helmet of Vietnamese soldiers, usually green.
An item used for sleeping, hung between two trees, very familiar to soldiers in the jungle.
A bamboo stick that helped soldiers march steadily across the majestic Truong Son mountains.
The call by Uncle Ho for the entire population to stand up and fight the French in 1946.
Large-scale and simultaneous attacks on all fronts to achieve final victory.
A state where there is no war and the people live in happiness.
The historical event when South and North Vietnam were reunited in 1975.
The temporary boundary dividing South and North Vietnam after 1954.
A legendary trail running along the Truong Son range, used to transport food and weapons to the South.
A peace document signed in 1954, ending the war with the French colonists.
An important document signed in 1973, forcing the US military to withdraw from Vietnam.
A large building in Saigon that witnessed the complete liberation of Southern Vietnam on April 30, 1975.
The river flowing through the 17th parallel, where the Hien Luong Bridge once divided the country.
